fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to build out that autoscaler config toolkit we've been talking about. Basically the idea is that ops people shouldn't have to write all this boilerplate parsing and merging code every time they stand up a new environment. The system needs to handle loading settings from files or directories (similar to how we handled the multi-file approach in the deploy pipeline thing we did before), merging layered configs where later values win, and translating those scheduler policy documents into something the scaling engine can actually consume. There's also some canonicalization step where we fill in sensible defaults — I think the built-in metric source and target names matter here but I don't remember the exact names off the top of my head, check the existing code. One tricky part: when the built-in metric source is active and the query looks like a short form (no slashes in it), we need to expand it somehow using group and job info. Validation also needs to happen and report multiple errors at once rather than stopping at the first one. The output needs to go to stdout in a consistent line-by-line format. Editor temp files should be ignored when scanning directories. Please make sure the architecture is clean — separate concerns properly, don't dump everything in one file.

trigger_keywords: what does the system produce with no input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default configuration emits these exact values: log_json=false, log_level=info, plugin_dir_suffix=/plugins, default_evaluation_interval_ns=10000000000, nomad_address=http://127.0.0.1:4646, http_bind_address=127.0.0.1, http_bind_port=8080, apm_plugins=nomad-apm:nomad-apm, target_plugins=nomad-target:nomad-target. Each on its own line, newline-terminated.

trigger_keywords: same plugin name in two layers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Plugin entries with the same Name field are replaced by the later layer's declaration. Plugin entries with distinct names are both retained. In the output, plugins within each category are sorted alphabetically by name and rendered as name:driver with optional args and config fields appended.

trigger_keywords: which files are ignored in a directory scan
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When loading from a directory, only .hcl files are considered eligible. Editor temporary files (e.g., files with a ~ suffix or other non-.hcl extensions) are ignored. Eligible files are sorted by name alphabetically and merged in that order.

trigger_keywords: what happens when path is missing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the path does not exist or is marked as missing, the output is exactly: error=path_not_found\n

trigger_keywords: what is the built-in source identifier
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The built-in metric source name is 'nomad-apm'. The built-in scaling target name is 'nomad-target'. These are the defaults applied during policy canonicalization when source or target name fields are empty.

trigger_keywords: how is the nomad-apm query expanded with job and group
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Query expansion applies only when: (1) the source is 'nomad-apm' (the built-in metric source), AND (2) the query string does not contain a slash character. When both conditions hold, the query is expanded to include the group and job identifiers from the target config. The keys used from target config are 'group' (mapped from Group) and 'job_id' (mapped from Job).

trigger_keywords: how are scheduler target attributes mapped to plugin keys
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During canonicalization, scheduler target fields are copied into the plugin-facing target config under lowercase keys: 'Job' becomes 'job_id', 'Group' becomes 'group'. The original keys (Job, Group, Namespace) are also retained in the config map. New keys are only added when they are not already present.

Validation reports all errors at once (not fail-fast). The required fields are: id (non-empty string), min (must be present/non-zero context: missing means the key was absent), target (the top-level target block), and policy (the nested policy block). A null input produces error=policy_missing. An empty id string produces error=id_missing. Errors are emitted in this order: id_missing, min_missing, target_missing, policy_missing. A valid policy emits: validation=accepted, id, min, max, query, strategy name.

trigger_keywords: how are nested HTTP and Nomad config fields merged
does a partial nested object overwrite the whole thing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Nested objects like HTTP and Nomad are merged field-by-field: only non-zero/non-empty fields from a later layer overwrite the corresponding field in the accumulated config. A layer that only specifies BindPort does not erase a BindAddress set by an earlier layer.

trigger_keywords: what happens with malformed optional blocks during translation
how are config values rendered in translated policy
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Policy translation is best-effort: malformed optional blocks (target, strategy) are omitted rather than causing an error. Config values inside target and strategy blocks are rendered as strings regardless of original type (e.g., int 2 becomes '2', bool true becomes 'true'). Target attributes from the top-level scheduler target are merged into target config. An empty input policy document produces: id=, min=0, max=0, enabled=true, source=, query=, evaluation_interval_ns=0, target=<nil>, strategy=<nil>.

trigger_keywords: what happens when canonicalize receives null
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the input to canonicalization is null/nil, the output is exactly: policy=<nil>\n. A non-null but empty policy object {} receives all defaults: enabled=false, source=nomad-apm, evaluation_interval_ns=10000000000, target_name=nomad-target, target_config={}, strategy_name=, strategy_config={}.

trigger_keywords: what is the default timing value
how many nanoseconds is the default interval
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default evaluation interval is 10 seconds expressed as 10000000000 nanoseconds. This value is applied both in the default runtime configuration and during policy canonicalization when no evaluation interval is present.

trigger_keywords: how are args and config rendered for plugins
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each plugin category line is formatted as: category_plugins=name1:driver1,name2:driver2 args=[...] config={key=value,...}. Args appear as args=[arg1,arg2] only when present. Config appears as config={key=value} with keys sorted alphabetically, only when present. Plugins within the line are sorted by name. Example: apm_plugins=influx-db:influx-db,nomad-apm:nomad-apm,prometheus:prometheus args=[all-the-encryption] config={address=http://prometheus-new.systems:9090}

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

show me feature1 default runtime configuration test cases
what are the feature1 default runtime configuration test inputs
give me the feature1_default_runtime_configuration test file

what does feature1 default runtime configuration expect
test cases for feature2 layered configuration merge
show me feature2 layered configuration merge test cases

give me the feature2_layered_configuration_merge test file

what does feature2 layered configuration merge expect
test cases for feature3 configuration file loading
show me feature3 configuration file loading test cases
what are the feature3 configuration file loading test inputs
give me the feature3_configuration_file_loading test file
feature3 configuration file loading expected output
what does feature3 configuration file loading expect
test cases for feature4 scaling policy translation
show me feature4 scaling policy translation test cases
what are the feature4 scaling policy translation test inputs
give me the feature4_scaling_policy_translation test file
feature4 scaling policy translation expected output
what does feature4 scaling policy translation expect
test cases for feature5 scaling policy canonicalization
show me feature5 scaling policy canonicalization test cases
what are the feature5 scaling policy canonicalization test inputs
give me the feature5_scaling_policy_canonicalization test file

test cases for feature6 scaling policy validation
show me feature6 scaling policy validation test cases
what are the feature6 scaling policy validation test inputs

feature6 scaling policy validation expected output

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled the multi-file approach in the deploy pipeline thing we did before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3 (Configuration File Loading): load a single .hcl file or scan a directory, sort eligible .hcl files by name, merge them in order, ignore editor temp files and non-.hcl extensions — as specified in feature3_configuration_file_loading.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: check the existing code for the built-in metric source and target names
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The built-in APM plugin name is 'nomad-apm' and the built-in target plugin name is 'nomad-target', used as defaults in Feature 1 output and Feature 5 canonicalization logic

context_pointers.vague_reference: expand it somehow using group and job info
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5 canonicalization: when source=='nomad-apm' and query contains no slash, expand the query by appending group and job_id values sourced from the target config map keys 'group' and 'job_id'

context_pointers.vague_reference: that canonicalization step where we fill in sensible defaults
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5 (Scaling Policy Canonicalization): fills default_evaluation_interval_ns=10000000000, target_name=nomad-target, source=nomad-apm, empty config maps, and copies Job->job_id / Group->group into target config when those keys are absent

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please refer to the existing scheduler integration patterns in the codebase and align with the stdout contract format already established for other features — each field on its own line as key=value, newline-terminated.

feature1_default_runtime_configuration.j.description: The default configuration supplies stable runtime, connection, health endpoint, timing, and built-in plugin values when no user configuration is provided.

nomad_address=http://127.0.0.1:4646
http_bind_address=127.0.0.1
http_bind_port=8080
apm_plugins=nomad-apm:nomad-apm
target_plugins=nomad-target:nomad-target

feature2_layered_configuration_merge.jso.description: Later configuration layers override scalar values, nested endpoint fields merge field-by-field, and plugin declarations with the same name are replaced while distinct plugins are retained.

log_json=true
log_level=trace
plugin_dir=/var/lib/nomad-autoscaler/plugins

feature3_configuration_file_loading.json.description: Configuration loading accepts a single file or a directory of configuration files, merges directory files in name order, ignores editor temporary files, and reports missing or malformed inputs as normalized errors.

feature4_scaling_policy_translation.json.description: A scheduler scaling policy document is translated into the internal autoscaling policy shape using best-effort conversion of durations, booleans, limits, target settings, and strategy settings.

strategy_name=strategy
strategy_config={bool_config=true}

min=0
max=0
enabled=true
source=
query=
evaluation_interval_ns=0
target=<nil>
strategy=<nil>

feature5_scaling_policy_canonicalization.description: A translated autoscaling policy is canonicalized by adding defaults, mapping scheduler target fields into plugin target fields, and expanding short built-in metric queries when applicable.
